Do you think it is smart for me to use Just Devin local as my Architect and Executor? Should I be concerned about context lengths or is there ways to get around that limitation?


Thoughts
You're right to be concerned. Using a single Devin instance as both Architect and Executor will definitely hit context length issues. Here's my assessment:

Problems with Single-Instance Approach
Context Accumulation:

Architect read order alone: AI_HANDOFF.md + PRINCIPLES + ARCHITECT_PATTERNS + OR_RULES + ARCHITECTURE + PLANS + LANDMINES + DEBT = potentially 2000+ lines per workflow invocation
Log Reading adds: execution logs, CHANGELOG, attestation files, traces, source files
Round Table adds: plans, briefs, panelist responses, scorecards
Research adds: web search results, research log
All this accumulates in a single conversation
After 5-10 plans, you'll be hitting token limits or getting degraded responses.

Workarounds (and their limitations)
1. Selective Reading (designed into system)

Already in AI_HANDOFF.md: "Executor reads only listed rules, not full documents"
Architect could read only relevant sections per workflow
Limitation: Still accumulates over time, and governance docs grow
2. File-Based State (already designed)

The system is built around file-based governance (PLANS.md, CHANGELOG, etc.)
Architect reads fresh context per workflow from files
Limitation: Doesn't help with conversation-level context growth
3. Subagents

run_subagent can spawn fresh context agents for specific tasks
Could spawn a subagent for Executor work while main session acts as Architect
Limitation: Adds complexity, coordination overhead
Better Approach
Separate Sessions + File-Based Coordination:

Use one Devin session as Architect (short-lived, per batch of plans)
Use separate sessions for Executor (per plan execution)
Coordinate via files: Architect writes plans to plans/, Executor reads and executes
Re-read governance docs per session (GR15 already requires this)
Or Use the Workflow as Designed:

The AI_HANDOFF.md assumes Architect is a stateless web chat for this exact reason
Design the system around file-based state, not conversation state
Accept that you'll need to paste context or re-read docs between sessions
My Recommendation
Don't try to force everything into one session. Instead:

Architect session: Use for plan drafting, Round Table review, log analysis. Keep it focused. End session when plans are delivered.
Executor session: Execute plans one at a time. Use the /open, /verify, /close skills as designed.
Coordination: Let the file system be the SSOT, not the conversation history.
The governance docs you've created are actually well-designed for this - they emphasize file-based state over conversation state for exactly this reason.

Would you like me to help you set up a workflow that leverages separate sessions effectively?

no I will use seperate chats with seperate context lengths

The AI_HANDOFF.md governance is built around this exact pattern - file-based state coordination between separate sessions, with the Architect treating the chat transcript as ephemeral and relying on governance documents as the source of truth.

Option 1: Split Governance (Recommended)
Keep separate constitutions for each role:

AGENTS.md → Rename to EXECUTOR.md (Executor Constitution)

AI_HANDOFF.md → Process coordination between the two

Clear separation of concerns
Each role has its own invariants and session protocol
Executor-specific rules (execution traces, attestation, STOP conditions) stay with Executor
Architect-specific rules (GR rules, workflow compliance, research scope) stay with Architect
Cons:

More files to maintain
Need to keep both in sync when process changes
Option 2: Unified AGENTS.md
Put both roles in AGENTS.md with sections:



markdown
# AGENTS.md — Agent Constitutions
 
## Architect Constitution
- Architect invariants
- Architect session protocol
- Architect GR rules
 
## Executor Constitution  
- Executor invariants
- Executor session protocol
- Executor operations rules

Single source for both roles
Easier to see relationship between roles
Cons:

File gets larger
Mixing concerns (Architect doesn't need execution trace rules, Executor doesn't need GR rules)

Current Problem
AI_HANDOFF.md is 950+ lines mixing:

Round Table Workflow

Recommended Structure: Granular Workflows


.agent/architect/
├── ARCHITECT_CONSTITUTION.md      # Architect invariants, GR rules
├── RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md           # Research workflow (Ask mode)
├── LOG_READING_WORKFLOW.md        # Log analysis workflow (Ask mode)
├── ROUND_TABLE_WORKFLOW.md        # Plan review workflow (Plan mode)
└── ARCHITECT_PATTERNS.md          # Patterns reference
 
.agent/executor/
├── EXECUTOR_CONSTITUTION.md       # Executor invariants (from AGENTS.md)
├── EXECUTOR_WORKFLOW.md           # Execution workflow (Code mode)
├── OR_RULES.md                    # Operational rules
├── ARCHITECTURE.md                # Architecture constraints
└── ARCHITECT_PATTERNS.md          # (symlink or copy)
Mode Mapping
Role	Workflow	Devin Mode	Purpose
Researcher	RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md	Ask	External research, no changes
Log Reader	LOG_READING_WORKFLOW.md	Ask	Analyze execution logs, draft rules
Planner	ROUND_TABLE_WORKFLOW.md	Plan	Draft/review plans, no execution
Executor	EXECUTOR_WORKFLOW.md	Code	Execute plans, make changes
Benefits
Focused context: Each session loads only what it needs
Clear purpose: One workflow per activity type
Mode alignment: Maps naturally to Ask/Plan/Code
Easier updates: Change research workflow without touching execution
Better reuse: Can invoke specific workflows independently
File Migration Plan

Split AI_HANDOFF.md into:
RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md (Section 7)
LOG_READING_WORKFLOW.md (Section 5)
ROUND_TABLE_WORKFLOW.md (Section 6)
Move AGENTS.md → EXECUTOR_CONSTITUTION.md
Create EXECUTOR_WORKFLOW.md (session protocol from AGENTS.md)
Update cross-references between files

Log reading would be Review workflow right?


Thoughts
You're right to question that. Looking at the activities:

Log Reading Workflow (Section 5):

Analyzes execution logs after plans complete
Identifies what went wrong
Drafts rules to prevent recurrence
Creates fix plans
Round Table Workflow (Section 6):

Reviews plans before execution
Expert panelist evaluation
Finds issues in plan design
Improves plan quality
These are different types of "review":

Log Reading = Post-execution review (what happened?)
Round Table = Pre-execution review (will this work?)
So you could call them:

POST_EXECUTION_REVIEW_WORKFLOW.md (or LOG_ANALYSIS_WORKFLOW.md)
PRE_EXECUTION_REVIEW_WORKFLOW.md (or ROUND_TABLE_WORKFLOW.md)
Or collapse them into a single REVIEW_WORKFLOW.md with two sections?

My recommendation: Keep them separate. They're triggered at different times, have different purposes, and require different context. Log Reading needs execution logs and traces; Round Table needs plans and briefs.

What do you prefer - separate workflows or combined?

Searched web for Devin custom subagents vs AGENTS.md when to use each
Thoughts
Based on the search results, the Devin-native approach is different from separate sessions. Here's what the docs show:

Devin's Native Pattern
AGENTS.md = Always-on rules for ALL sessions (auto-loaded at start) Custom Subagents = Specialized worker profiles (explicitly invoked when needed) Skills = Reusable capabilities (invoked on demand)

Recommended Devin Structure


SovereignAI/
├── AGENTS.md                          # General project rules (always-on)
├── .devin/
│   ├── agents/
│   │   ├── architect/
│   │   │   └── AGENT.md              # Architect subagent profile
│   │   ├── executor/
│   │   │   └── AGENT.md              # Executor subagent profile
│   │   ├── researcher/
│   │   │   └── AGENT.md              # Researcher subagent profile
│   │   └── reviewer/
│   │       └── AGENT.md              # Reviewer subagent profile
│   └── skills/
│       ├── architect/
│       │   └── SKILL.md              # Architect workflow invocation
│       ├── executor/
│       │   └── SKILL.md              # Executor workflow invocation
│       └── ...
└── .agent/
    ├── architect/                    # Governance docs (read by subagents)
    └── executor/                     # Governance docs (read by subagents)
How It Works
Main session uses AGENTS.md (general rules)
When needed, main session delegates to custom subagent:
"Use architect subagent to review this plan"
"Use executor subagent to run this plan"
Each subagent has its own AGENT.md with role-specific instructions
Skills provide workflow logic that subagents can invoke
Benefits
Native to Devin's architecture
Subagents have separate context windows (solves your context problem)
Explicit delegation (clear when to use which role)
Cost control (can pin cheaper models for specific subagents)
Should I restructure to use custom subagents instead of separate session types?

So the devin setup is basically the same as Sovereign AI with Orchestrator but it does not have managers?


Thoughts
Looking at the SovereignAI architecture from the document titles, you're essentially right:

SovereignAI Architecture:

Orchestrator (coordinates everything)
Department Managers (manage specific departments - Coding, Education, Library, Research)
Workers/Specialists (do actual work within departments)
Devin Architecture:

Main session (acts as orchestrator)
Custom subagents (specialized workers - architect, executor, researcher, reviewer)
No explicit manager layer
The key difference is the manager abstraction layer:

SovereignAI Manager layer handles:

Department-level coordination
Resource allocation within department
Worker spawning and management
Cross-department communication
Devin skips this layer:

Main session → subagents (direct delegation)
Subagents are workers, not managers
Main session does all coordination
Implications:

Aspect	SovereignAI	Devin
Coordination	3-tier (Orchestrator → Manager → Worker)	2-tier (Main → Subagent)
Specialization	Department-level managers	Direct to specialized subagents
Complexity	Higher (more layers)	Lower (flatter)
Scalability	Better for large departments	Better for simple delegation
For your use case, Devin's 2-tier approach is probably sufficient since you're managing 4 roles (Architect/Executor/Researcher/Reviewer), not complex departments with many workers.

Should we proceed with the flatter Devin architecture, or do you think you need the manager layer for some reason?

Do a quick websearch to see if this is the best way to do it. Also can i chat to each of the subagents directly or will it always be through one main orchestrator?


Thoughts
Searched web for Devin subagent direct chat vs delegation main orchestrator
Thoughts
Based on the search results, you cannot directly chat with subagents. Here's what the docs show:

Subagent Communication Pattern
Main agent always orchestrates:

Main agent spawns subagents for specific tasks
Subagents run in their own conversation chain
You do not see the subagent's raw output directly
When subagent finishes, main agent summarizes results for you
Main agent pauses (foreground) or continues (background) while subagent works
Direct chat is not supported:

Subagents are designed for delegation, not direct conversation
You ask main agent: "Use architect subagent to create a plan"
Main agent spawns architect subagent, waits for result, summarizes
You never see the raw subagent conversation
This Changes the Architecture
Since you can't directly chat with subagents, your original separate sessions approach is actually better:

Approach	Pros	Cons
Separate sessions	Direct control, full context visibility, natural conversation	Manual coordination, no automatic orchestration
Subagents	Automatic orchestration, parallel execution	Indirect communication, summarized output only, always through main agent
Recommendation
Stick with separate sessions rather than subagents for your use case because:

You want direct control over each role
You need to see full context, not summarized results
You want natural conversation with each agent type
Your 4-role pattern fits separate sessions better
The workflow files approach (PLAN_WORKFLOW.md, REVIEW_WORKFLOW.md, etc.) with separate sessions is the right path.

Should I proceed with the file-based workflow reorganization?

okay so how should I set up the different agents. Which ones should I use?


Thoughts
Based on the search results and our discussion, here's the best setup for separate session types:

Recommended Setup: Directory-Scoped AGENTS.md
Devin supports directory-scoped AGENTS.md - it automatically applies rules based on file location. We can use this for role-based sessions:



SovereignAI/
├── AGENTS.md                          # General project rules (root level)
├── sessions/
│   ├── architect/
│   │   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Architect-specific rules + workflow instructions
│   │   └── .agent/                    # Architect governance (soft link to main .agent/architect/)
│   ├── executor/
│   │   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Executor-specific rules + workflow instructions
│   │   └── .agent/                    # Executor governance (soft link to main .agent/executor/)
│   ├── researcher/
│   │   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Researcher-specific rules + workflow instructions
│   │   └── .agent/                    # Research governance (if needed)
│   └── reviewer/
│       ├── AGENTS.md                  # Reviewer-specific rules + workflow instructions
│       └── .agent/                    # Review governance (if needed)
└── .agent/                            # Main governance docs
    ├── architect/
    └── executor/
How It Works
When you start a session:

Navigate to directory: cd sessions/architect/
Start Devin - it loads that directory's AGENTS.md automatically
AGENTS.md contains role-specific instructions + workflow reference
Devin's directory scoping:

Root AGENTS.md: Applies to all files (always-on)
Directory AGENTS.md: Applies when working in that directory
Devin auto-discovers AGENTS.md files throughout workspace
Alternative: Skills-Based Approach
Or use skills for role invocation:



.devins/skills/
├── architect/
│   └── SKILL.md                      # "Act as Architect" instructions
├── executor/
│   └── SKILL.md                      # "Act as Executor" instructions
├── researcher/
│   └── SKILL.md                      # "Act as Researcher" instructions
└── reviewer/
    └── SKILL.md                      # "Act as Reviewer" instructions
Usage: "Invoke architect skill" → Session acts as Architect

My Recommendation
Directory-scoped AGENTS.md because:

Native Devin capability (no skill invocation needed)
Clear physical separation (different directories = different roles)
Automatic loading (just cd to directory and start)
Simpler (no skill management overhead)
Which approach do you prefer?

Okay how are we going to handle this its a drastic framework change. Also what is the point of Architect in this framework?


Thoughts
You're absolutely right to question this. Let me reconsider the role definitions.

Current Architect Role (from AI_HANDOFF.md)
Architect is a container for multiple activities:

Log Reading - Analyze execution logs, draft rules
Round Table - Review plans with panelists
Research - External research
Plan Creation - Draft plans
Problem with "Architect" in Devin Context
Since Devin can do everything (read/write files, execute commands), the Architect vs Executor separation doesn't make sense anymore. That separation was designed for:

Architect = Web chat (no file access, planning only)
Executor = Devin CLI (full access, execution only)
Revised Role Structure
If we remove "Architect" as a role, we get:



sessions/
├── planner/          # Creates plans (was part of Architect)
├── executor/         # Executes plans (existing)
├── researcher/       # External research (was part of Architect)
└── reviewer/         # Reviews logs/plans (was part of Architect)
Or Even Simpler
Some of these might not need separate sessions:

Planner + Executor: Could be same session with plan-then-execute workflow
Researcher: Could be invoked on-demand (not a dedicated session)
Reviewer: Could be part of planner workflow
Framework Change Strategy
You're right - this is a drastic change. We should:

Pause and define requirements first: What do you actually need?
Incremental migration: Keep existing governance, adapt incrementally
Test with real usage: See which session types you actually use
Question for you: What's your actual workflow? Do you need separate role sessions, or could this be simpler with fewer session types?

Ok so how will we handle the reviewer to planner role as the reviews create a plan for cleaning up the executors dirty coding work


Thoughts
Good point. The "Reviewer" in this context is really a Planner who creates fix plans based on execution logs.

Simplified Structure


sessions/
├── planner/          # Creates ALL plans (new plans + fix plans from log review)
├── executor/         # Executes plans
└── researcher/       # On-demand external research
Workflow
Normal cycle:

Planner session: Create original plan → Save to plans/
Executor session: Execute plan → Generate execution log
Planner session: Read execution log → Create fix plan → Save to plans/
Executor session: Execute fix plan → Generate execution log
Repeat until clean
Research (on-demand):

Researcher session: Investigate X → Provide findings to Planner
Why This Works
Planner handles all plan creation: Whether from user requirements or log analysis
Executor handles all execution: Same workflow regardless of plan type
Researcher is context-heavy: Clean slate for each research task
No "Reviewer" role needed: It's just Planner with different input
File Structure


sessions/
├── planner/
│   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Planner instructions + workflow reference
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── PLAN_CREATION.md       # How to create plans from requirements
│       └── LOG_REVIEW.md          # How to create fix plans from execution logs
├── executor/
│   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Executor instructions + workflow reference
│   └── workflows/
│       └── CODE_EXECUTION.md      # How to execute plans
└── researcher/
    ├── AGENTS.md                  # Researcher instructions + workflow reference
    └── workflows/
        └── RESEARCH.md            # How to conduct research
This gives you 3 session types instead of 4, with clear responsibilities. Does this make sense?

No I like reviewer because we can set up specific rules and gates for it to check against whilst reducing the bloat on other agents


Thoughts
Good point. The reviewer serves a distinct purpose with its own governance.

Final Structure


sessions/
├── planner/          # Creates plans (from requirements or reviewer findings)
├── executor/         # Executes plans
├── reviewer/         # Reviews execution logs, checks against rules/gates
└── researcher/       # External research
Workflow
Planner: Create original plan → Save to plans/
Executor: Execute plan → Generate execution log
Reviewer: Read execution log → Check against rules/gates → Identify issues → Report findings
Planner: Read reviewer findings → Create fix plan → Save to plans/
Executor: Execute fix plan → Generate execution log
Reviewer: Review fix plan execution → Check against rules/gates
Repeat until clean
Why Separate Reviewer Works
Specific rules/gates: Reviewer has its own governance for checking execution quality
Reduced bloat: Planner focuses on plan creation, Executor on execution, Reviewer on validation
Clear separation: Each role has one primary responsibility
Specialized context: Reviewer loads validation rules, not planning or execution rules
File Structure


sessions/
├── planner/
│   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Planner instructions + workflow reference
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── PLAN_CREATION.md       # How to create plans from requirements
│       └── FIX_PLAN_CREATION.md   # How to create fix plans from reviewer findings
├── executor/
│   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Executor instructions + workflow reference
│   └── workflows/
│       └── CODE_EXECUTION.md      # How to execute plans
├── reviewer/
│   ├── AGENTS.md                  # Reviewer instructions + workflow reference
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── LOG_REVIEW.md          # How to review execution logs
│       └── RULE_CHECKING.md       # Rules and gates to check against
└── researcher/
    ├── AGENTS.md                  # Researcher instructions + workflow reference
    └── workflows/
        └── RESEARCH.md            # How to conduct research
This gives you 4 specialized roles with clear responsibilities. Ready to proceed with the reorganization?

Can I use gates for all different agents?


Thoughts
Yes, gates work well for all agents. They provide structured verification and quality control for each role.

Gates Per Agent
Planner Gates:

Gate P1: Plan structure check (header, Executor Manifest, sections)
Gate P2: Rule compliance check (referenced rules exist in governance)
Gate P3: Landmine screen (no blocking landmines in plan)
Gate P4: Brief format check (if Round Table workflow)
Gate P5: Plan size limit (≤120 lines unless override)
Executor Gates:

Gate E1: /open skill verification (workspace ready)
Gate E2: Step execution verification (after each step)
Gate E3: /verify skill pass (all checks green)
Gate E4: Execution trace entry (appended after each action)
Gate E5: Attestation completion (before /close)
Gate E6: Final verification pass (verify_execution.py --final)
Reviewer Gates:

Gate R1: Log read complete (execution log, CHANGELOG, attestation)
Gate R2: Governance read complete (OR_RULES, ARCHITECTURE, LANDMINES)
Gate R3: Rule gaps identified (missing or broken rules)
Gate R4: Findings documented (severity, evidence, fix)
Gate R5: Compliance posting complete (every finding logged)
Researcher Gates:

Gate R1: Scope defined (question, boundaries, success criteria)
Gate R2: Search executed (queries run, results retrieved)
Gate R3: Authority verified (sources meet threshold)
Gate R4: Findings documented (appended to research log)
Gate R5: Success criteria met (research question answered)
Implementation
Each session's AGENTS.md would include:

Role-specific instructions
Gate definitions
Gate compliance posting requirements
Stop conditions (gate failure = halt)
This gives you structured verification across all agents, not just Executor. Should I include this in the reorganization?

should they? what does documentation say about the best way to do it?


Thoughts
Searched web for Devin AGENTS.md location best practices directory structure
Thoughts
Based on the documentation, here's what Devin supports:

AGENTS.md Location Options
1. Project Root (Recommended)



SovereignAI/AGENTS.md                    # Global project rules
Loaded at session start for all files
Recommended approach for project rules
2. Subdirectories (Directory-Scoped)



SovereignAI/frontend/AGENTS.md          # Frontend-specific rules
SovereignAI/backend/AGENTS.md           # Backend-specific rules
Loaded lazily when agent accesses files in that directory
Keeps context focused on relevant rules
3. .devin/agents/ (Custom Subagent Profiles)



.devins/agents/reviewer/AGENT.md        # Subagent profile
For custom subagent profiles (note: AGENT.md, not AGENTS.md)
Used when main agent delegates to subagents
Different from session-based AGENTS.md
What This Means for Your Structure
Since you're using separate sessions (not subagents), options are:

Option A: Directory-Scoped (sessions/)



sessions/planner/AGENTS.md
sessions/executor/AGENTS.md
Navigate to directory → Devin loads that AGENTS.md
Natural for separate session workflow
Option B: Governance Directory (.agent/)



.agent/planner/AGENTS.md
.agent/executor/AGENTS.md
Consistent with existing governance structure
But might not be discovered by Devin's AGENTS.md loading
Option C: Root with Profiles



AGENTS.md                                # Global rules
.agent/roles/planner.md                  # Role profiles (not AGENTS.md)
Root AGENTS.md references role profiles
But loses directory-scoped benefit
Which approach aligns better with how you'll actually use the sessions?

Which approach is going to work best in devin local?


or does it not matter?


Thoughts
It doesn't really matter functionally. Devin will discover and load AGENTS.md files regardless of which directory they're in, as long as you navigate to that directory before starting the session.

Functionally identical:

sessions/planner/AGENTS.md → Navigate to sessions/planner/ → Start Devin → Loads that AGENTS.md
.agent/planner/AGENTS.md → Navigate to .agent/planner/ → Start Devin → Loads that AGENTS.md
Organizational choice:

Approach	Pros	Cons
sessions/	Clear separation: "sessions" = where you work	Creates new top-level directory
.agent/	Consistent with existing governance structure	Mixes governance with session instructions
My recommendation: Use .agent/ since:

You already have .agent/architect/ and .agent/executor/
The new roles are essentially governance/session instructions
Keeps everything in one place
sessions/ might be confusing (are these actual sessions or just configuration?)
But honestly, either works. Which do you prefer?

what is the point of the . before .agent?


??


Thoughts
The . before .agent is a Unix/Linux convention for hidden directories. Files and directories starting with a dot are hidden by default in file listings and are typically used for configuration or system files.

On Windows, this doesn't have special meaning - it's just a naming convention carried over from Unix systems. Windows doesn't hide dot-prefixed files by default (unless configured to do so).

Common patterns:

.git/ - Git configuration
.devin/ - Devin configuration
.agent/ - Your agent governance
.env - Environment variables
.vscode/ - VS Code settings
For your project:

.agent/ signals "configuration/governance, not source code"
But functionally, agent/ would work the same on Windows
It's just organizational convention
So the question is: Do you want to follow the convention (.agent/) or use a more visible name (agent/ or agents/)?

is the workflow folders optimum or should we just use AGENTS.MD for specific rules?


Thoughts
Good question. Let's consider the tradeoffs:

Separate workflow files:

AGENTS.md = role definition + gates
workflows/PLAN_CREATION.md = specific workflow
Pros: Separation of concerns, easier to reference specific workflows
Cons: More files, more complexity, multiple file loads
Single AGENTS.md:

AGENTS.md = role definition + gates + all workflows
Pros: Simpler, fewer files, everything in one place
Cons: Larger file, less separation
Given Devin's context management:

AGENTS.md is designed as "always-on context" for a directory
Fewer file loads = better for context
Simpler structure = easier to maintain
My recommendation: Put everything in AGENTS.md. The workflows can be sections within AGENTS.md. This is simpler and more aligned with how Devin uses AGENTS.md.

Example:



markdown
# Planner Session
 
## Role
Create plans...
 
## Gates
- Gate P1: ...
 
## Workflow: Plan Creation
Steps for creating plans from requirements...
 
## Workflow: Fix Plan Creation  
Steps for creating fix plans from reviewer findings...
Should I consolidate into single AGENTS.md files and remove the workflows folders?

Thoughts
Searched web for Devin AGENTS.md file structure single vs multiple files code quality token cost
Thoughts
Based on the search results and your priority (Code Quality > Token Cost Reduction > Efficiency), here's what the documentation shows:

Key findings:

Devin docs explicitly recommend: "Rules and AGENTS should be kept as small as possible" and "Skills are only injected into context when relevant"
Research paper: AGENTS.md reduces runtime by 28.64% and token consumption by 16.58% - but this is for a single root-level file
Design constraints: 32 KiB default cap (~8,000 tokens), files concatenate from root down (linear growth with directory depth)
Best approach for your priorities:

Keep separate workflow files because:

Code Quality (Priority #1): Devin docs explicitly recommend keeping AGENTS.md small and using skills/files for conditional context
Clear separation of concerns improves maintainability
Each file stays focused and readable
AGENTS.md = role definition + gates (small)
Workflow files = detailed procedures (loaded as needed)
If we consolidate:

AGENTS.md would become large (role + gates + 2 workflows)
Violates Devin's "keep AGENTS.md small" recommendation
Harder to maintain and update
Current structure is optimal:



Agents/planner/
├── AGENTS.md                    # Small: role + gates
└── workflows/
    ├── PLAN_CREATION.md         # Detailed workflow
    └── FIX_PLAN_CREATION.md     # Detailed workflow
Should we keep the current structure with separate workflow files?

Yes okay. In what order should we create our workflow? which agent is best to start with?


Thoughts
Good question. Considering the dependencies and your priority (Code Quality > Token Cost > Efficiency), here's the recommended order:

Recommended Order
1. Executor First

Most operational agent (actually does work)
Existing AGENTS.md foundation to build on
Can test with existing plans immediately
Clear success criteria (plan executes → verify → commit)
Most defined constraints (skills, gates, existing structure)
2. Reviewer Second

Validates Executor output (we'll have test data from Executor)
About quality control (aligns with Code Quality priority)
Has clear gates and rules to check against
Dependent on Executor having execution logs to review
3. Planner Third

Creates the plans that Executor executes
Needs understanding of what Executor requires
More complex (two workflows: original + fix plans)
Benefits from seeing Executor/Reviewer patterns
4. Researcher Last

Most independent (doesn't depend on other agents)
On-demand usage (not core workflow chain)
Simplest scope (just external research)
Can be refined based on actual research needs
Why This Order?
Bottom-up: Build the execution foundation first, then build planning around it
Testable: Each step produces artifacts the next step can use
Feedback loop: We can refine Executor based on actual usage, then build Reviewer around that, etc.
Ready to start with Executor workflow?

is that okay or will it cause issues?


Thoughts
On Windows specifically, it's probably okay because Windows file systems are case-insensitive. Most tools will treat app and App the same.

Potential issues:

Inconsistency: Having Agents/, Logs/, Plans/ but app/ looks inconsistent
Cross-platform: If you ever deploy to Linux/macOS, case matters and could break imports
Git: Git on Windows can sometimes have issues with case renames
Python imports: Depending on how imports are structured, they might be case-sensitive
Recommendation: Since it's locked by a process, leave it as app/ for now. You can:

Close any running Python processes/VS Code and try the rename later
Accept the inconsistency temporarily
Or we could lowercase everything back to logs/ and plans/ for consistency
Functionally on Windows, it won't break anything. It's more of a code style/consistency issue.

Would you prefer to:

Leave it mixed case for now
Revert Logs/Plans back to lowercase to match app/
Try to close processes and capitalize app later
yes i want app to be App

Does .venv need to be in root or can it be located in a Dependencies folder? actually research dont just presume


Thoughts
Searched web for Python virtual environment location best practices root vs dependencies folder
Thoughts
Based on the research, both approaches are valid according to Python best practices:

Root location (current - .venv/):

Pros: Convention, IDEs expect it, easier activation, tool support
Cons: Clutters root, but it's gitignored anyway
Python docs: "conventionally named .venv or venv in the project directory"
Central location (Dependencies folder):

Pros: Cleaner project directory, disposable environments kept separate
Cons: Requires configuration in IDEs/tools, need to remember activation path
Python docs: "under a container directory for lots of virtual environments, such as ~/.virtualenvs"
Key findings:

Virtual environments are disposable and should be gitignored
Both locations are "predictable places" per best practices
IDEs like VS Code expect .venv in root by default
You can reproduce environments from requirements.txt anywhere
Recommendation: Keep .venv/ in root because:

It's the standard convention
Better tool support (VS Code, etc.)
It's already gitignored
Moving it would require IDE reconfiguration
Should we keep .venv/ in root or move it to a Dependencies folder?

can you reconfigure the IDE to change the location?


Thoughts
I can help reconfigure the IDE, but I need to know which IDE you're using and confirm the approach.

If using VS Code:

Create .vscode/settings.json with Python interpreter path
Set python.defaultInterpreterPath to the new location
Example: "python.defaultInterpreterPath": "Dependencies/.venv/Scripts/python.exe"
If using PyCharm:

Configure in Project Settings → Python Interpreter
Point to the new virtual environment location
If using other IDE:

Need to know which one to provide specific steps
Questions:

Which IDE are you using?
Do you want me to create the IDE configuration files?
What would you name the folder - Dependencies/, Env/, or something else?
Once I know this, I can:

Move .venv/ to the new location
Create the appropriate IDE configuration files
Test that the configuration points to the right location
What IDE are you using?

We are in Devin Local


Devin IDE Desktop


Thoughts
Searched web for Devin IDE Desktop Python interpreter configuration virtual environment location
Thoughts
Searched web for Devin Desktop custom Python interpreter path location configuration
Thoughts
Based on the research, Devin Desktop works differently from traditional IDEs. It uses managed environments and blueprints rather than configurable interpreter paths.

Key findings:

Devin Desktop uses environment blueprints and snapshots for Python setup
The official templates show .venv in the root directory as the standard
Configuration is done via .devin/config.json for project settings, but this is for MCP servers and permissions, not Python interpreter paths
Devin Desktop doesn't appear to support custom Python interpreter paths like VS Code or PyCharm
Implications:

Moving .venv/ to a Dependencies/ folder might break Devin Desktop's environment setup
Devin Desktop expects the standard .venv/ location based on their templates
There's no documented way to configure a custom Python interpreter path in Devin Desktop
Recommendation: Keep .venv/ in root because:

Devin Desktop templates expect .venv/ in root
No documented support for custom interpreter paths
Moving it could break Devin Desktop's environment management
It's the standard convention and already gitignored
Devin Desktop is designed as a managed environment (like Codespaces), not a configurable local IDE. Should we keep .venv/ in root for compatibility?

should we test?


?

Move .venv/ to Dependencies/ folder
Try running a simple Python command in Devin Desktop to see if it still finds the environment
If it breaks, move it back

Lets keep it there. Can other things go in there as well like pyproject.toml git ignore and gi attribues and .mypy_cache


Thoughts
Good question, but some of these must stay in root for Git to work properly:

Must stay in root:

.gitignore - Git expects this in the repository root. Moving it will break Git ignore functionality.
.gitattributes - Git expects this in the repository root. Moving it will break Git attributes functionality.
Can move to Dependencies/:

.mypy_cache - This is just a cache directory. Moving it would clean up the root and work fine.
Should stay in root (probably):

pyproject.toml - Many Python tools expect this in the project root. Moving it might break tooling (pip, setuptools, IDEs, etc.). Could be risky.
Recommendation: Move .mypy_cache to Dependencies/ (safe cleanup) Keep .gitignore, .gitattributes, and pyproject.toml in root (Git/Python tooling requirements)

Okay now that we have a fresh repo. lets take a quick look at the absolute best ways to setup the agents. we want to use hard gating with scripts for checks. to enforce scope and efficiency. We want to use the github MCP for git commands now that we have it. lets do a websearch to see the best way to set this up starting with executor


Thoughts
Searched web for AI agent hard gating scripts enforce scope efficiency best practices
Thoughts
Searched web for Devin Local gating scripts hooks PreToolUse scope enforcement
Thoughts
Excellent research! Here are the best practices for hard gating with scripts:

Key Findings
1. PreToolUse Hooks (Devin Native)

Best approach: Devin's .devin/hooks.v1.json with PreToolUse hooks
Mechanism: Scripts receive event data on stdin, return exit code 2 + stderr to block
Benefits: Native Devin support, deterministic, can provide feedback to agent
2. Deterministic Gating Patterns

ScopeGate approach: Everything not explicitly allowed is denied (fail-closed)
ThumbGate approach: Hard-block dangerous patterns, warn risky ones, auto-approve routine actions
Multi-layer: Static checks + context-aware evaluation
3. GitHub MCP Integration

Devin supports MCP tools and hooks can target them with matchers like mcp__github__*
Can gate specific GitHub operations (create_issue, push, etc.)
Recommended Executor Structure


Agents/executor/
├── AGENTS.md                  # Executor role + gates
├── workflows/
│   └── CODE_EXECUTION.md      # Execution workflow
└── scripts/                   # Gating scripts
    ├── check_scope.sh         # Path and command scope checks
    ├── check_git_ops.sh       # Git operation validation
    ├── check_file_edit.sh     # File edit scope enforcement
    └── verify_gate.sh         # Final verification gate
Hook Configuration (.devin/hooks.v1.json):



json
{
  "PreToolUse": [
    {
      "matcher": "exec",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command",
          "command": "Agents/executor/scripts/check_scope.sh"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "matcher": "edit",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command", 
          "command": "Agents/executor/scripts/check_file_edit.sh"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "matcher": "mcp__github__",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command",
          "command": "Agents/executor/scripts/check_git_ops.sh"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
Script Pattern:

Read JSON from stdin (tool_name, tool_input)
Check against policy
Exit 0 (allow) or 2 (block with stderr message)
Should I create this structure and start building the Executor gating scripts?

Adopt Plan 35 as-is (maximum quality, higher cost/complexity)

Thoughts
Excellent research! Here's the summary for the best approach:

AGENTS.md Best Practices Summary
Key Findings:

Most Important Category: Architecture & Design rules (99% of developers rank as most important, but most under-represented in practice)
Highest ROI Section: Commands (executable commands with full flags)
Critical Structure: Three-tier Boundaries (Always do / Ask first / Never do)
Five-Category Framework:
Architecture & Design (most important)
Commands & Operations
Boundaries & Permissions
Testing & Verification
Project Structure & Context
Common Mistake: Files dominated by code style rules when architecture/design failures are most damaging.

Best Practices:

Start with Architecture & Design rules, not code style
32 KiB limit, aim for under 150 lines
Use exact executable commands with full flags
Focus on structural boundaries and scoping constraints
For Planner Agent
Based on this research, the best approach is:

Architecture & Design Rules (adapted from Architect GR rules):

Plan structure requirements (header, manifest, sections)
Quality gates (completeness, clarity, specificity)
Integration with Devin Interactive Planning
Commands:

How to create plans, where to save them, how to reference design docs
Boundaries:

Always do: Read design docs, follow structure rules
Ask first: Scope changes, architectural decisions
Never do: Skip governance rules, create invalid plans
Testing: Plan validation, completeness checks

This suggests the Hybrid Approach from our menu - adapt key GR rules (especially structure/compliance) combined with Planner-specific Devin integration rules.

Searched web for AI agent rule file indexing best practices large rule files efficiency
Thoughts
Excellent research! The findings strongly support your indexing idea:

Key Research Findings:

Per-turn cost: Rules loaded on EVERY turn are expensive (large files consume massive context)
On-demand reads: External files read via tool calls are paid once per session (much cheaper)
Indexing pattern: repo-rules-agent uses discover → extract → index → query pattern for large rule sets
Trigger-based: Grep-able triggers fire rules automatically vs requiring agent memory
Small summary + external detail: Put index/summary in rule file, verbose content in external files
Your approach aligns with research:

Index at top → sections below → query scoped to task
Cache files for conversation reuse
Avoid reading whole rule files
Implementation decision:

Thoughts
Based on the research findings:

Research shows:

AGENTS.md should be ~100 lines as a pointer map (not encyclopedia)
Progressive disclosure: index should be lightweight, detailed content in sections
JIT (Just-In-Time) indexing: provide paths/pointers, NOT full content
Rule of thumb: if document exceeds 500 lines, split with summary at top
Best practice: Quick reference tables with minimal info (Task | Command | Notes)
My recommendation: Option A (Minimal index) - Research supports lightweight indexes with just Rule ID | Trigger | Section. Detailed summaries in the index defeat the purpose of progressive disclosure and make the index itself bloated.

Thoughts
Searched web for "old_string not found" edit error prevention file editing best practices
Thoughts
Based on the research, the specific "old_string not found" error is caused by:

Main Causes:

Whitespace mismatches (tabs vs spaces, CRLF vs LF line endings)
Read tool format issues (line number separators causing tab counting errors)
File state conflicts (IDE modifications, auto-save delays)
Large old_string blocks (higher probability of whitespace errors)
Solutions from research:

Use smaller, targeted edits (avoid large old_string blocks)
Read file immediately before editing
Save IDE files before editing
Use Write tool for full file replacement when Edit fails repeatedly
Best approach given Quality > Token Cost > Efficiency:

Question skipped
Thoughts
Searched web for Devin Local file editing best practices error prevention "old_string not found"
Thoughts
Based on Devin Local specific research, the error is a known file state desync issue even when content is byte-identical. Key findings:

Devin Local Specific:

Multi-line edits (≥3 lines) fail more often than small edits (≤2 lines)
File state cache desync causes intermittent failures
<str_replace> tool requires EXACT match including whitespace
Write tool is more reliable for large changes
Solutions from research:

Read immediately before every edit
Use small edits (≤2 lines) for reliability
Use Write tool for large/multi-line changes
Retry with re-read if edit fails
Question skipped
Thoughts
Based on the error we encountered:

Error Type: Edit tool matching error Cause: Exact string matching failure - the old_string I tried to match didn't exist exactly as expected in the file, likely due to:

Whitespace/formatting differences
Large multi-line match attempts are harder to match exactly
Content drift between what I expected vs actual file content
This is not Devin-specific - it's a general file editing issue affecting any tool using exact string matching (Devin, Claude Code, etc.).

Question skipped
Thoughts
You're absolutely right. Let me analyze what actually caused the error:

Root Cause Analysis: I attempted to replace a large multi-line block (the entire table with 15 rows + summaries) in one edit operation. The exact string matching failed due to the complexity of matching that much content precisely.

What worked: I switched to small, targeted edits (editing just the table header, then individual rows one at a time) and those succeeded.

This matches the research finding: Multi-line edits (≥3 lines) fail more often than small edits (≤2 lines) due to exact matching complexity.

Solution: Use small, targeted edits instead of large block replacements.

Thoughts
Based on research, AGENTS.md uses "nearest-wins" - only the closest AGENTS.md to the file being edited is automatically read. This means:

If we have:

Root AGENTS.md (universal rules)
Agents/planner/AGENTS.md (planner-specific)
Agents/executor/AGENTS.md (executor-specific)
When working in Agents/planner/, only Agents/planner/AGENTS.md is read automatically.** Root AGENTS.md is NOT automatically read unless explicitly referenced.

Thoughts
Based on research, here's the best practice recommendation:

Research Findings:

Planner-Executor-Reviewer Pattern: Planner breaks down goals, Executor runs steps, Reviewer checks output
Reviewer Role: "Reviewers are second-pass agents -- they don't create, they evaluate"
ACS SOP: Reviewer reviews design, code, tests. Executor designs within approved scope, implements, self-tests
Single-Responsibility: Reviewer should NOT create plans, only review and evaluate
Recommendation (Research-Backed):

Reviewer: Creates reviews/findings only (no plan creation)
Planner: Creates all plans (original, fix, scan)
Unified Workflow: Planner should handle all plan types in single workflow

Based on research findings, here's the best option analysis:

Phase-based plans: Each phase needs verification and definition of done
Checkpoints: Natural review points at each phase
Single source of truth: Plans are persistent, reviewable artifacts
Sizing rules: Split large plans into phases to keep LLM execution focused
Best Option: Option C (Phase-based single file) - Research supports this because:

Phase-based structure matches Plan-Export-Verify pattern
Single file provides clarity and avoids complexity
Each phase has verification gates (matches research)
Natural checkpoint system (matches research)
Simpler than sub-workflows but more structured than minimal

Based on Devin CLI documentation, there's no built-in support for managing multiple separate sessions with different agent contexts. The /handoff command is for transferring to cloud sessions, not managing local agent sessions.

Can I not just have different Agents open?


User uploaded image 1
Thoughts
Yes, you can absolutely have different AGENTS.md files open in your IDE! This is actually a simple and effective approach.

How it would work:

Keep all agent AGENTS.md files open in separate tabs/windows
When you want to use a specific agent, reference its AGENTS.md in your prompt
The context from the open file provides the agent's rules and workflows
For your multi-agent system:

Open Agents/planner/AGENTS.md when doing Planner work
Open Agents/executor/AGENTS.md when doing Executor work
Open Agents/reviewer/AGENTS.md when doing Reviewer work
Open Agents/researcher/AGENTS.md when doing Researcher work
Benefits:

Simple - no complex session management needed
Fast - just switch tabs or reference the right file
Visual - you can see all agent configurations at once
No additional setup required
Limitation:

Devin CLI will still use the "nearest" AGENTS.md file based on your working directory (nearest-wins rule), so you'd need to be in the right directory or explicitly reference the specific AGENTS.md file in your prompts.
This is actually a much simpler solution than trying to implement complex session management! Would this approach work for your workflow?

Can I simply say Open Planner workflow?


Thoughts
Yes! You can create simple commands to switch between agent workflows. Here are a few approaches:

Option 1: Shell Functions (Simplest)



bash
# Add to your shell profile
planner() { cd C:/SovereignAI/Agents/planner; }
executor() { cd C:/SovereignAI/Agents/executor; }
reviewer() { cd C:/SovereignAI/Agents/reviewer; }
researcher() { cd C:/SovereignAI/Agents/researcher; }
Then just type: planner or executor

Option 2: Devin CLI Skills Create a skill that handles agent switching:

open-planner skill → changes directory and loads Planner context
open-executor skill → changes directory and loads Executor context
Option 3: Batch Scripts (Windows)



batch
@echo off
cd C:\SovereignAI\Agents\planner
echo Planner workflow activated
Save as planner.bat, then just type planner

Option 4: Use Devin's built-in directory awareness Since AGENTS.md uses nearest-wins, you could simply:

Type cd Agents/planner when you want Planner
Type cd Agents/executor when you want Executor
Devin automatically reads the nearest AGENTS.md
My recommendation: Option 1 (shell functions) or Option 4 (simple cd commands) - both are simple and work with the AGENTS.md nearest-wins behavior we already have.

Which approach sounds most intuitive for your workflow?

Command Write-Output in /C:/SovereignAI

Write-Output @'
# Scan Workflow
 
**Version**: 1.0  
**Last Updated**: 2026-07-22  
**Status**: Active
 
## Purpose
Create fix/scan plans for the SovereignAI project based on Reviewer findings or periodic maintenance cycles. Scanner plans address issues, errors, or governance violations identified during plan execution or review.
 
## Workflow Overview
```
Reviewer Findings → Scan Plan Creation → Scan Plan Delivery → Round Table → Executor
```
 
## Phase 1: Review Input Assessment
 
**Trigger**: Reviewer provides findings, errors, or governance violations  
**Goal**: Understand the scope and severity of issues requiring fix/scan plan
 
**Steps**:
1. **Read Reviewer Report**: Read the review findings document provided by Reviewer agent
2. **Assess Severity**: Categorize findings by severity (critical, high, medium, low)
3. **Scope Impact**: Determine which files/components are affected by findings
4. **Check Dependencies**: Identify any dependencies or ripple effects of fixes
5. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-1 PASS: {findings_count} findings assessed, severity: {summary}`
 
**Exit Gate**: Reviewer findings fully understood and categorized
 
---
 
## Phase 2: Root Cause Analysis
 
**Trigger**: Review input assessment complete  
**Goal**: Identify root causes of issues to prevent recurrence
 
**Steps**:
1. **Investigate Root Causes**: For each finding, identify the underlying cause
2. **Pattern Detection**: Look for patterns or systemic issues across findings
3. **Governance Check**: Check if findings indicate governance rule violations
4. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-2 PASS: {root_cause_count} root causes identified, patterns: {summary}`
 
**Exit Gate**: Root causes identified for all critical/high findings
 
---
 
## Phase 3: Fix Strategy Design
 
**Trigger**: Root cause analysis complete  
**Goal**: Design effective fix strategies for identified issues
 
**Steps**:
1. **Strategy Selection**: Choose appropriate fix strategy for each finding
2. **Alternatives Considered**: Document rejected alternative strategies and reasoning
3. **Consequence Analysis**: Assess consequences of fix approach vs alternatives
4. **Risk Assessment**: Identify risks associated with fix implementation
5. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-3 PASS: {strategy_count} fix strategies designed, risks: {summary}`
 
**Exit Gate**: Fix strategies designed for all findings with risk assessment
 
---
 
## Phase 4: Scan Plan Creation
 
**Trigger**: Fix strategy design complete  
**Goal**: Create comprehensive scan plan following PR1-PR15 and GR1-GR5
 
**Steps**:
1. **Plan Header**: Create plan header with Vision principles, PR rules, and resolved questions (PR1)
2. **Path Convention**: Use repo-relative paths only, no host-local paths (PR2)
3. **Manifest Integration**: Include complete Executor Manifest section (PR5)
4. **Plan Structure**: Follow defined structure: header, manifest, phases, deliverables (PR6)
5. **Quality Gates**: Ensure plan passes completeness, clarity, and specificity checks (PR7)
6. **Landmine Screening**: Screen plan against governance landmines (PR8)
7. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-4 PASS: Scan plan created, gates: {pass_count} PASS`
 
**Exit Gate**: Scan plan created and passes all quality gates
 
---
 
## Phase 5: Reviewer Integration
 
**Trigger**: Scan plan created  
**Goal**: Ensure scan plan addresses all Reviewer findings comprehensively
 
**Steps**:
1. **Finding Coverage**: Verify all Reviewer findings are addressed in scan plan
2. **Action Mapping**: Map each finding to specific plan phases/actions
3. **Verification Gates**: Include verification gates for each fix
4. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-5 PASS: {findings_count} findings addressed, coverage: 100%`
 
**Exit Gate**: All Reviewer findings comprehensively addressed in scan plan
 
---
 
## Phase 6: Scan Plan Delivery
 
**Trigger**: Reviewer integration complete  
**Goal**: Deliver scan plan to Round Table for coordination
 
**Steps**:
1. **Plan Validation**: Final validation of scan plan completeness
2. **Handoff Documentation**: Create handoff document for Round Table
3. **Compliance Posting**: Ensure all compliance lines are present and valid
4. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-6 PASS: Scan plan ready for Round Table, phases: {phase_count}`
 
**Exit Gate**: Scan plan delivered to Round Table
 
---
 
## Periodic Scan Plans (Every 5 Plans)
 
**Trigger**: Every 5 completed plans in the plan sequence  
**Goal**: Periodic maintenance and governance compliance check
 
**Steps**:
1. **Scan Trigger**: Automatically trigger scan workflow at plan 5, 10, 15, etc.
2. **Comprehensive Review**: Conduct comprehensive review of recent plan executions
3. **Governance Check**: Verify governance rules are being followed correctly
4. **Quality Assessment**: Assess overall quality of recent plan executions
5. **Issue Identification**: Identify any patterns or systemic issues
6. **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate SCAN-PERIODIC PASS: Periodic scan at plan {plan_number}, issues: {issue_count}`
 
**Exit Gate**: Periodic scan plan created and delivered to Round Table
 
---
 
## Universal Rules Compliance
 
**All phases must follow**:
- **GR1-GR5**: Universal governance rules (agent responsibilities, single-responsibility, handoff boundaries)
- **ER1-ER5**: Universal editing rules (file editing best practices, large changes, failure recovery)
- **PR1-PR15**: Planner-specific rules (plan creation, structure, quality gates)
- **PR16**: Universal rules integration
 
---
 
## Stop Conditions
 
**Halt execution if**:
- Missing compliance line for any gate
- Scan plan fails to address critical Reviewer findings
- Root cause analysis incomplete for critical findings
- Fix strategy lacks risk assessment
- Plan fails quality gates (PR7)
- Plan contains governance landmines (PR8)
 
---
 
## Evolution
 
**This workflow evolves when**:
- New types of Reviewer findings emerge
- Scan plan patterns change
- Governance rules are updated
- Periodic scan frequency changes
- Workflow integration points change
'@ | Out-File -FilePath "Agents/planner/workflows/SCAN_WORKFLOW.md" -Encoding UTF8

Searched web for Devin Local skills directory navigation vs context loading best practices
Thoughts
Based on the research, here's my recommendation:

Best Approach: Combined Approach

Why:

AGENTS.md Discovery: Devin uses "nearest-wins" for AGENTS.md based on working directory. Navigating to the agent directory ensures proper AGENTS.md loading.
Skill Injection: Skills inject prompts inline, so they can both change directory AND load context in a single skill invocation.
Supporting Files: Skills can include references to agent files (AGENTS.md, rules, workflows) that get loaded when invoked.
Subagent Option: If you want clean context separation, skills can run as subagents with independent context windows.
Skill Structure:



.devin/skills/
├── planner/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Navigate to Agents/planner/ + load context
├── executor/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Navigate to Agents/executor/ + load context  
├── reviewer/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Navigate to Agents/reviewer/ + load context
└── researcher/
    └── SKILL.md        # Navigate to Agents/researcher/ + load context
Each skill would:

Navigate to the respective agent directory
Read and load the agent's AGENTS.md
Load universal rules from ../shared/UNIVERSAL_RULES.md
Load agent-specific rules and workflows
Set up the context for that agent's work
Question skipped
Thoughts
Based on the research, here's how AGENTS.md discovery works:

AGENTS.md Discovery Rules:

Project Root: Devin finds the project root by looking for .git or .jj directory, walking up from your current working directory
Nearest-Wins: If you have nested directories with AGENTS.md files, the one closest to your current working directory takes precedence
Auto-Discovery: AGENTS.md files are automatically discovered and loaded by Devin when you're working in that directory
How this applies to our structure:



SovereignAI/                    # Root - could have root AGENTS.md
├── Agents/
│   ├── planner/
│   │   └── AGENTS.md          # Loaded when working in Agents/planner/
│   ├── executor/
│   │   └── AGENTS.md          # Loaded when working in Agents/executor/
│   └── ...
Example:

If you run cd Agents/planner and then start working, Devin will automatically load Agents/planner/AGENTS.md
If you run cd Agents/executor, Devin will automatically load Agents/executor/AGENTS.md
The skill /Planner would essentially do cd Agents/planner for you, and Devin automatically discovers the AGENTS.md in that directory
So the navigation approach works because Devin's AGENTS.md discovery is directory-based, not command-based.

Searched web for Devin Local AGENTS.md manual loading explicit context injection without directory change
Thoughts
Excellent research! This explains the problem perfectly. Based on the findings:

Key Discovery:

Root AGENTS.md: "Always-on" - applies to all files in the project
Subdirectory AGENTS.md: "Glob rule" - only applies when accessing files inside that directory
No manual loading: AGENTS.md discovery is entirely location-based, there's no manual loading mechanism
This means:

If you're working in Plans/ (root level), Agents/planner/AGENTS.md won't activate because you're not accessing files in Agents/planner/
Directory navigation doesn't help you stay in your actual work directory
Solution: Skills for Context Loading Skills are perfect for this because they:

Inject prompts inline - can load AGENTS.md content regardless of directory
Support supporting files - can reference rules, workflows, etc.
Don't require directory changes - you stay in Plans/, App/, etc.
Recommended Skill Structure:



.devin/skills/
├── planner/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Loads Agents/planner/AGENTS.md + rules + workflows
├── executor/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Loads Agents/executor/AGENTS.md + rules + workflows  
├── reviewer/
│   └── SKILL.md        # Loads Agents/reviewer/AGENTS.md + rules + workflows
└── researcher/
    └── SKILL.md        # Loads Agents/researcher/AGENTS.md + rules + workflows
Each skill would explicitly read and inject the agent's context, letting you work anywhere (Plans/, App/, etc.) while having the right agent active.

Thoughts
Good question! Based on the research, IDE navigation works differently:

How IDE Navigation Affects AGENTS.md:

Clicking on folder in IDE: Generally does NOT activate the AGENTS.md unless you're actually accessing/working with files in that directory
Opening files in that directory: WILL activate the AGENTS.md for that directory (since you're accessing files inside it)
Nearest-wins rule: The AGENTS.md closest to the file you're working with takes precedence
Example:

If you click on Agents/planner/ in the IDE file tree → No activation (just viewing directory)
If you open Agents/planner/AGENTS.md to edit it → Planner AGENTS.md activates (accessing file in that directory)
If you work on Plans/plan-30.md → Root AGENTS.md applies (if it exists), no subdirectory AGENTS.md activates
This confirms the problem:

Working in Plans/ won't activate any agent's AGENTS.md
You'd need to open files in Agents/planner/ to activate planner context, which is not practical
Skills are the right solution:

Skills can load agent context regardless of where you're working
You can stay in Plans/, App/, etc. and use /Planner to load planner context
This gives you explicit control over which agent is active

Searched web for Devin Local AGENTS.md root folder activation directory depth behavior
Thoughts
Based on the research, here's how AGENTS.md activation works with directory depth:

AGENTS.md Activation Rules:

Root AGENTS.md: "Always-on" - applies to ALL files in the project, loaded at session start
Subdirectory AGENTS.md: "Glob rule" - ONLY applies when working with files inside that specific subdirectory
Opening root folder: Does NOT activate subdirectory AGENTS.md files
Working in subdirectory: Activates that subdirectory's AGENTS.md only when accessing files in it
Answer to your question:

If we put AGENTS.md one layer deeper (in Agents/ subdirectory), it will NOT activate when working in root
Opening the root folder will NOT activate subdirectory AGENTS.md files
Subdirectory AGENTS.md only activates when you're actually accessing/editing files in that specific subdirectory
This is perfect for our conflict problem!

Solution:

Keep our current structure: Agents/planner/AGENTS.md, Agents/executor/AGENTS.md, etc.
These will NOT activate when you're working in Plans/, App/, etc. at root level
They will ONLY activate if you open files in Agents/planner/, Agents/executor/, etc.
Skills can still load agent context manually when you want to switch agents
This eliminates the conflict! You can:

Work in Plans/ with no automatic AGENTS.md activation
Use /Planner skill to load planner context manually
Only get automatic planner context if you open files in Agents/planner/ (which you shouldn't need to do often)
Should we proceed with this approach?

Thoughts
Based on the research, AGENTS.md does NOT need to be in a folder named "agents". Here are the key findings:

AGENTS.md Requirements:

File name: Must be AGENTS.md or agents.md (case insensitive)
Folder name: NO requirements - can be any folder name
Location: Can be in any directory or subdirectory
Discovery: Automatically discovered throughout workspace and subdirectories
Your proposed structure will work:



Agents/
├── planner/
│   └── agents/                    # Folder name doesn't matter
│       └── AGENTS.md              # Only activates when accessing files in Agents/planner/agents/**
├── executor/
│   └── context/                   # Could name it anything
│       └── AGENTS.md              # Would work the same

Agents/planner/agents/AGENTS.md → Only activates when accessing files in Agents/planner/agents/**
Skills can still manually load this AGENTS.md file regardless of folder name

Question skipped
Thoughts
Searched web for AI agent findings tracking best practices persistent storage JSON vs SQLite rule creation
Thoughts
Based on the research, here are the best practices for AI agent findings tracking:

Research Findings Summary:

Academic Research:

"Beyond Session JSON" paper: SQLite is often the best default for local/single-node agents because it provides transactions, write-ahead logging, constraints, and queryability without operational burden
"Interchange Is Not Storage" paper: Plain JSON is useful for interchange, export, debug snapshots, but SQLite is stronger for durable systems with audit, concurrency, crash recovery
Key insight: JSON file rewrites give no database-style notion of commit record or recovery procedure
Practical Implementations:

planning-with-sqlite: Replaces 3-file markdown pattern with normalized SQLite database via MCP
OpenCode: Uses relational SQLite storage with Drizzle ORM for structured session data
Sherpa AI: Recommends SQLite for production, JSON only for development
Comparison:

Aspect	JSON	SQLite
Transactions	❌ No	✅ ACID compliant
Crash recovery	❌ No commit records	✅ Write-ahead logging
Queryability	❌ File-based search	✅ SQL queries + indexing
Concurrency	❌ File locking issues	✅ Built-in locking
Audit trail	❌ Limited	✅ Structured history
Schema evolution	❌ Manual	✅ Migration support
Question skipped
Thoughts
Searched web for SQLite database human review accessibility vs JSON files AI agent quality token efficiency
Thoughts
Based on the research, here's the analysis for your priorities (Quality > Token Cost > Efficiency):

SQLite Accessibility:

Human Review: SQLite databases are single files accessible via GUI tools (DB Browser for SQLite, DBeaver, etc.)
Portability: "Want to back up your AI's entire memory? Copy one file" - very portable
Export Options: Can export to JSON/CSV for git tracking and human readability
Query Access: Can write SQL queries for custom review views
Token Efficiency Impact:

Sub-1ms latency: SQLite achieves sub-1ms query latency vs 25-500ms for cloud databases
Token savings: Research shows >90% token savings with summary-first retrieval using SQLite
Scale: SQLite+FTS5 handles 4,300+ memories with sub-1ms latency on local NVMe
Network overhead: Zero network latency since it's a local file
Quality Impact:

ACID compliance: Provides transactions, write-ahead logging, constraints
Crash recovery: Has commit records and recovery procedures (JSON lacks this)
Data integrity: Structured schema ensures data quality vs ad-hoc JSON structure
Audit trail: Built-in queryable history vs manual file parsing
Accessibility Solutions:

Create human-readable views in SQLite
Export to JSON for git tracking
Use MCP tools for friendly interfaces
GUI tools make it accessible to non-technical users

Thoughts
Searched web for AI agent findings tracking database schema design best practices SQLite
Thoughts
Based on the research, here are the SQLite schema design best practices:

Research Findings:

STRICT tables for control data to ensure type safety and validation
Minimal viable schema - start with core tables and extend as needed
Dual-table pattern - separate content from operations/indices
Flexible workspace - database should be flexible coordination mechanism, not rigid contract
Incremental complexity - add features as needed rather than over-engineering upfront
For our Round Table findings use case:

Start with minimal viable schema (findings + rules tables)
Use STRICT for validation (findings severity, rule status)
Add complexity incrementally (patterns, proposals, validation stages)

Searched web for workflow design single responsibility vs integration complexity best practices
Thoughts
✅ Gate A1 PASS: Web search completed for workflow design single responsibility vs integration complexity best practices

Gate A6: Web Search Findings Summary:

Integration Layer Responsibility: Integration layers should handle coordination, not business logic - business rules belong in the owning system
Clear Ownership Boundaries: Each system should remain authoritative in its domain without leaking logic into integration layers
Centralized Workflow Coordination: Workflows should be centralized for coordination to avoid scattered logic and unclear ownership
Single Responsibility in Integration: Integration should act as a bridge, not a merger - each layer handles its own responsibility
Workflow-Centered Boundaries: Workflow-centered boundaries exist primarily to coordinate business processes across domain services
Support for Our Decision: The BP findings support integrating panelist capture directly into PLAN_WORKFLOW.md rather than as a separate workflow. This centralizes coordination in the planner workflow while maintaining clear ownership boundaries, avoiding scattered logic across multiple workflow files.

AGENTS.md
+1
- **Gate A6**: Always summarize web search findings before presenting questionnaire options
- **Gate A7**: When making workflow design decisions that improve the system, suggest adding a rule to AGENTS.md with a gate to ensure consistent workflow improvement

Based on my analysis of the old AI_HANDOFF.md Round Table workflow vs our current PLAN_WORKFLOW.md, here are the key differences:

Missing Components in Current Workflow
1. Pre-Round Table Preparation (Old Steps 1-2.5)
Old Workflow:

Step 1: Re-Read governance documents (LANDMINES.md, PRINCIPLES.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, OR_RULES.md) with individual gates
Step 2: Screen plans against LANDMINES.md, assemble brief with 9 sections, assemble material, draft rubric-based prompt
Step 2.5: Architect Self-Check against 6-dimension rubric + ARCHITECT_PATTERNS.md, fix self-identified findings
Current Workflow:

Phase 6 jumps directly to panelist intake without governance document re-reading, plan screening, brief assembly, or self-check
2. Panelist Scoring & Performance Tracking (Old Step 5)
Old Workflow:

Score each panelist 1-100 with weighted acceptance criteria
Check findings against ARCHITECT_PATTERNS.md for pattern recurrence
Create persistent score documents for long-term panelist optimization
Current Workflow:

Phase 6 only collects verdicts (Pass/Conditional/Block) without panelist scoring
No pattern recurrence checking
No long-term panelist performance tracking
3. Clean Pass Gate (Old)
Old Workflow:

Score thresholds: 90-100 (clean pass), 70-89 (review with rationale), <70 (block)
Verification checklist for critical criteria
STOP conditions with specific thresholds
Current Workflow:

Phase 6 only has basic quality gates (70/100 confidence, 4/6 panelists)
No comprehensive clean pass gate with score thresholds
4. Governance Amendment Process (Old Step 8)
Old Workflow:

Creates governance plans when new ARCHITECT_PATTERNS identified but no Executor execution exists
Governance plans run before batch execution (Phase 0)
Separate governance plan scope (only ARCHITECT_PATTERNS.md, score docs, AI_HANDOFF.md)
Current Workflow:

Phase 8 integrates rules directly without separate governance plan process
No Phase 0 governance plan concept
5. Brief & Prompt Structure (Old)
Old Workflow:

Brief: 9 sections in order, ≤80 lines (Rev 1 only)
Rubric-based prompt with 6 evaluation dimensions
Panel-of-Experts pattern with Parallel Fan-Out
Current Workflow:

No brief structure defined
No rubric-based evaluation criteria
No prompt structure guidance
Current Workflow Advantages
1. Systematic Findings Tracking
SQLite database with audit trail (missing in old)
JSON export for git persistence (missing in old)
Pattern analysis with ML clustering (more advanced than old)
2. Agent Separation (GR3 Compliance)
Reviewer analyzes findings, Planner integrates rules (clearer responsibility)
Old workflow had Architect doing both analysis and integration
3. Structured Data Extraction
Schema-based extraction with validation (more systematic than old)
Confidence scoring for extraction quality (missing in old)
Critical Gaps Summary
Missing from Current:

Governance document re-reading before Round Table
Plan screening against LANDMINES.md
Brief assembly with 9-section structure
Architect self-check against rubric + patterns
Panelist scoring (1-100) and performance tracking
Clean pass gate with score thresholds
Governance plan process for pattern updates
Rubric-based evaluation criteria
Added in Current:

SQLite findings database with audit trail
JSON export for git persistence
ML-based pattern analysis (frequency + clustering)
Agent responsibility separation (Reviewer vs Planner)
Schema-based extraction with validation
The current workflow has better technical infrastructure (database, export, ML) but lacks the rigorous preparatory steps and quality gates that made the old workflow systematic and thorough.

do a websearch and see BP and what we should integrate


Thoughts
Searched web for Round Table workflow panelist evaluation scoring best practices quality gates
Thoughts
Searched web for quality gates threshold scoring evaluation criteria rubric best practices
Thoughts
✅ Gate A1 PASS: Web search completed for Round Table workflow panelist evaluation scoring best practices quality gates

Gate A6: Web Search Findings Summary:

Panel Evaluation Best Practices:

Independent Scoring: Panelists must score independently before group discussion to prevent groupthink and anchoring bias
Defined Competencies: 3-5 core competencies mapped to individual panelists with topic-based (not time-based) evaluation
Structured Rubrics: Clear 4-point scales with concrete descriptors to eliminate subjective "gut-feel" scores
Quality Gates: Threshold-based decision frameworks (90-100 clean pass, 70-89 review with rationale, <70 block)
Divergence Analysis: Track feedback divergence to ensure independent thinking vs artificial convergence
Predictive Validity: Structured panels reach r = .57 predictive validity with .74 interrater reliability
Quality Gate Best Practices:

Threshold-Based Gates: Clear pass/fail criteria with no gray areas
Dimensional Scoring: Composite quality scores across multiple dimensions (relevance, completeness, depth, specificity, structure, groundedness)
Baseline Comparison: Compare results against established baselines for regression detection
Evidence-First Debriefing: Start with evidence, then score, then decide to reduce bias and anchoring
Narrow Gate Focus: Start with narrow, representative eval sets rather than comprehensive coverage
Key BP Principles for Integration:

Rubric-Driven Evaluation: Replace unstructured assessment with dimensional rubrics
Independent Panelist Scoring: Prevent groupthink through independent submission before visibility
Threshold-Based Quality Gates: Clear pass/fail criteria with defined score ranges
Competency-Based Panelist Assignment: Map panelists to specific evaluation competencies
Evidence-First Decision Making: Discuss evidence before scores to reduce anchoring bias
AGENTS.md
+20
- **Compliance**: Post `✅ Gate A2 PASS: Round Table evaluation follows BP scoring and quality gate principles`
 
**Evolution Condition**: Panelist performance data indicates need for rubric or threshold adjustments
 
**Evidence**: BP research shows structured panels reach r = .57 predictive validity with .74 interrater reliability when using independent scoring and clear rubrics

Searched web for soft gates vs hard gates AI agent implementation best practices
Thoughts
✅ Gate A1 PASS: Web search completed for AI panelist web search integration and soft vs hard gates

Gate A6: Web Search Findings Summary:

Web Search Integration for Panelists:

Grounding Benefits: Web search grounds LLM responses in real-time, citable sources, replacing stale training knowledge
Evaluation Accuracy: Search APIs improve source accuracy (92% F-score on SimpleQA) and citation quality
Context Engineering: Ranked, LLM-optimized excerpts deliver relevant tokens efficiently
Freshness vs Cost: Managed APIs provide freshness but cost per query; self-hosted requires infrastructure investment
Integration Patterns: Search + answer, tool-calling agents, hybrid with internal data retrieval
Community Consensus: Tavily recommended for entry point, Exa for complex technical queries
Soft vs Hard Gates:

Hard Constraints: Actions system cannot take regardless of instructions - implemented at tool level, cannot be overridden
Soft Constraints: Actions system is instructed not to take - implemented as rules in context, bypassable by adversarial input
Approval Gates: Actions can propose but require human confirmation - appropriate for high-consequence, low-frequency decisions
Dual-Gate Governance: Policy enforcement at both decision-time (authority, scope) and commit-time (parameters, runtime state)
Hard-Gated Verification: Catches 100% of corrupt episodes vs 0% for soft-only, with ~23ms latency overhead
Deterministic Gates: Pure function predicates on state that block forbidden writes before execution

Thoughts
Gate A6: Web Search Findings Summary for Hard Gate Enforcement:

Hard Gate Implementation BP:

Tool-Level Implementation: Hard constraints should be implemented at the tool level, not model level - they cannot be overridden by instructions, context, or reasoning
Precondition Gates: Deterministic predicates that check proposed actions against current state and block forbidden writes before execution
Pure Function Gates: g(tool_name, args, state) → {allow, reject} that run on write path before mutating tool calls
No Model Override: Hard gates cannot be bypassed by prompt injection, adversarial input, or reasoning chains that conclude override is justified
Zero False Positives: Hard-gated verification catches 100% of corrupt episodes with zero false positives
Dual-Gate Enforcement: Policy enforcement at both decision-time (authority, scope) and commit-time (parameters, runtime state)
Validation Scripts: Deterministic, read-only predicates that validate state before allowing changes
Question skipped
